---
title: "Unit 3 — Monte Carlo & Estimators"
subtitle: "Analytic vs Monte-Carlo expectations · estimating π in 2-D/3-D/n-D · the curse of dimensionality"
engine: markdown
---

## Overview

This unit is about estimating things by **sampling** rather than solving. You'll approximate an
expectation $\mathbb{E}[f(X)]$ two ways — analytically and by Monte Carlo — and estimate the
constant $\pi$ by rejection sampling in two, three, and $n$ dimensions. The payoff is seeing
the universal $O(1/\sqrt N)$ convergence of sampling estimators and the **curse of
dimensionality**: as dimension grows, acceptance collapses, so the same accuracy costs
exponentially more samples.

## Learning objectives

By the end of Unit 3 you will be able to:

- **Compute $\mathbb{E}[f(X)]$ analytically** and approximate it by Monte Carlo. *(Apply)*
- **Estimate $\pi$ by rejection sampling** in 2-D, 3-D, and $n$-D. *(Apply)*
- **Demonstrate $O(1/\sqrt N)$ convergence** and the curse of dimensionality. *(Analyze)*
- **Interpret** estimator variance vs sample size, and why higher dimensions are harder.
  *(Evaluate)*

## What you'll do

Two halves, each combining code and a short derivation:

- **(A) Monte-Carlo expectation** — for $X\sim U(-1,9)$ and a degree-4 polynomial $f$, estimate
  $\mathbb{E}[f(X)]$ by averaging samples and watch it converge to the analytic value, which
  you also derive (D1).
- **(B) Estimating $\pi$** — recover $\pi$ from the accepted-sample fraction in the unit circle
  (2-D), sphere (3-D), and $n$-ball, and derive the three estimator formulas (D2).

Then answer two interpretation questions about convergence and the curse. **Any correct sampler
passes** — the autograder checks convergence and distributional properties, never specific
draws — so **seed your RNG**.

## Prerequisites & connections

**Review first.** Expectation and variance; definite integration (for the analytic value);
NumPy array operations and random sampling with a seeded generator
(`np.random.default_rng`). Unit 0's seed policy.

**Connections.**

> The **seed policy** from Unit 0 is graded here as a Process spec — every stochastic check is
> reproducible. The sampling mindset you build (estimate by drawing, watch it converge) is the
> foundation for the **MCMC posterior sampling in Unit 5**.

## How it's graded

Three bundles, specifications not points. **Correctness** (core/gateway) mixes **autograded**
stochastic checks (convergence, distributional properties, floor-and-guard — never pinned
draws) with **human-graded derivations** (the analytic expectation D1 and the $\pi$ formulas
D2). **Interpretation** scores your CERL answers (Claim, Evidence, Reasoning, Limits) and
**Process** covers seeding, a cheap import, and a clean render. Any unmet bundle gets one
**revise-and-resubmit**; autograded resubmissions are unlimited until the deadline. Full policy
in [`GRADING.md`](../GRADING.md).

## What to submit

- Your completed **`hw.py`** — `f`, `monte_carlo_expectation`, `running_expectation`,
  `estimate_pi_using_circle` / `_sphere` / `_n_ball`, `number_of_orthants` (signatures
  unchanged; RNG seeded).
- Your **derivation write-ups** — the analytic expectation (D1) and the $\pi$ estimator
  formulas (D2).
- Your **rendered notebook** with the convergence / curse figures and interpretation answers
  (I1–I2).

*Submit via the course page — [submit mechanism TBD].*

## Deadline & revision window

- Due: **[PLACEHOLDER]**
- Post-feedback revision window: **[PLACEHOLDER]**

## Estimated effort

Roughly **5–7 hours [PROPOSED]** — the estimators are short to code; the time goes into the
$n$-D derivation, the convergence experiments, and the interpretations.
